speaker: interviewer
text: July 10, 1995. Continuing with Regina Goldstein. Interviewed by [? Iris ?] [? Lesky, ?] North Hills, California, USA, English, tape number four. Action.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regina, you talked about the names you'd given to Yad Vashem. Would you like to memorialize anybody on this tape right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to memorialize my mother, my birth mother and father, Schmuel and Hena Finkelstein. There are too many others. So I'll just-- and my-- my grandfather, David Rosenson, and my uncle, Chaim Rosenson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other stories that you have that you haven't shared yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. I told you, when we were coming to the United States, that the isolate you so that you don't-- after getting all the shots and everything, and you're not supposed to get sick. Well, I caught the mumps and gave them to my sister Gloria. Gave them to my sister Gloria.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the time that-- when they-- when they came to look us over periodically, we-- I would stay out of sight. But poor Gloria was still a little swollen when we were ready to go on the train to go to the ship. So we had these coats with hoods on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mom said to her, if you-- you have a toothache. You have a toothache. [LAUGHS] So luckily, we got on board. Nobody noticed that her face was-- was probably about a third larger than it should have been.

speaker: interviewer
text: In as much as your new family-- aunt Helen, uncle Harry, your new parents and your new sisters, Gloria and Betty-- were together then a lot. Did you ever discuss with them your feelings, your stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not really. Not till quite recently. I-- I don't know why. And we've spoken about it, as to why we didn't, and we have no answer. Because I didn't start, and they didn't start. And I thought they didn't want to, and they maybe thought-- no one questioned me. And I think they thought they were protecting me. And I-- I think that that's part of the reason a lot of us don't talk to each other. So it's only recently that-- mainly, I think my sister Betty and I talk a little bit about it. I have a couple of friends I've spoken to about it who are American-born. But-- and I-- we really didn't. And I'm sad that we didn't, but we still have a chance now. So we will, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you shared these stories with your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, yes. My-- my son was very interested once he found out. Because at the beginning, we didn't say that I was adopted. It was just-- not-- not for any specific reason. We were a family, and that was that. But then when we did start discussing the Holocaust, my children-- and my mother, Helen, wrote a book. So my sister Betty, who does most of the calling and talking, said to me, you better tell your kids because it's going to be in the book. So I-- although I had told stories about the war and the Holocaust, I had not specifically explained my particular story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sat them down, and I told them. And my son said to me, you know, mom, there were many times you left us alone with that memorial book of your town. Didn't you think that we would read it? And we have been waiting for you to tell us. What effect do you believe your experiences have had on the way you brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I hope that I have instilled in them a-- a love and a respect for-- for the Jewish people. Not so much the religion, but the tradition, the history, the-- the things that are-- that we as a people have gone through through the centuries and survived, so that-- that they know that they belong to a special group of people, from my point of view. And that whatever they do in life, that they should remember who they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you believe that your experiences changed your views, if any were changed about religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: There have been many times where I've questioned-- questioned God's existence. I-- sometimes something happens, and I say, you know, God is good. So probably somewhere deep inside me, there's a war going on about it. But I questioned His existence because so many terrible things happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And not that I expected Him to send a bolt of lightning to save, but why He-- why He didn't make it possible for-- for more people to either save themselves or to have a little bit more of the world see and admit what was happening, rather than this-- this aloneness of a people, where they had no one to turn to. And of course, there are other individual reasons where I say, why did he let this one die? Why did this one die?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no answer to it, I know. But I-- from time to time, I question His being. And probably had not any of this happened, I would have been raised in a semi-orthodox atmosphere and never questioned it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever questioned as to why you believe you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so. I guess not-- I hadn't thought about it deeply enough to-- philosophically to think about it. I find at times there is an anger in me because I did. There's an anger with me. I feel left behind. There's an anger that-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] that it happened the way it happened. But I don't think-- I never thought that there was some tremendous purpose in my being. I think that in a lot of us, it was chance, being in the right place at the right time rather than in the wrong place at the wrong time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Knowing you had survived when so many didn't, did you ever make a promise to yourself or to your loved ones?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] a promise? [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I think I promised not so much to them as to myself, never, never to forget the people who aren't here today, to talk about them to my children, if I'm lucky to have grandchildren, to my grandchildren, to anyone who'd listen. Because if we talk about them, they're not completely gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I may not have said this to anyone in particular, but I have said it to myself, that we cannot leave them behind in our memories where they cease to exist. And if we have the pictures and we talk about them, and not only the way they died, but do you remember Uncle Chaim made me red boots? Do you remember this one liked this? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that that's important, because it not only keeps them alive for me in a way, but it gives them a humanity where you say to your children, this was your-- this was your Zayde David. He died in the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If you make him come alive by the things you remember, they have him in a way that they wouldn't. Maybe a tiny bit, maybe they would have had him in the normal way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regina, we thank you for sharing your story, and we thank you for being a part of this testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a few moments and introduce a few members of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regina, would you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me a second. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regina, would you introduce your family members here today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my sister, Betty, and this is my husband, Hal. My son is in Chicago. My daughter's at work. So these are representing my loved ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hal, I'm going to start with you for a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: The stories that you have heard from your wife, what effect do you believe that they have had on your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very profound. It gave me much more insight into the culture that I was born into. I was not a very religious person, although my family once removed were-- were deeply religious. My father and his sisters were not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't get an awful lot of that. And it was perfunctory with me. You know, you went. You did your thing. I was bar mitzvahed.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I met Gina and got to know her, I got to know a lot more about it, because it was a contact with something that was kind of out of my normal thinking, you know, something that I really knew nothing of. And I got to know something of it and began to feel some emotion about it and understand that there was something more than just being 'born,' quote, unquote, and saying that you're Jewish. So I really did get something out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Betty, you are Gina's adopted sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Genie's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your feelings, knowing that you have some experiences of your own from the Holocaust, about your relationship to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Genie and me personally?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel very close to her. I think she and I are very close. And I've always felt that-- I think from the moment she came, I felt a special feeling. And I remember even-- I don't know where it was, in Lodz or Lublin. After a while I asked my mother-- I don't know if you remember-- if Genie could call mom and dad mom and dad, because I was so sensitive to the fact that--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. I forgot about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] we have to be here for each other. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And as far as-- maybe I'm going to answer something you didn't ask or don't wish to ask. But I feel that we are different. All these years when I got married and I had children, I wanted to be so American. And I wanted my children not to-- all I ever said was, we were in the war, and just-- but never everything. And now that I've reached the age of 56, I have come to realize that I think we have all been affected by it, that there is no-- and-- and the world doesn't even comprehend. It isn't something that it's over. It isn't over in here, and it isn't over in here. And the nightmares exist. And our reaction to everything, I think, is different. Because I see when I'm with my American friends how I will feel about something or respond, and they-- and there's a difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: We thank you both for being here as part of Regina's story. And we're going to break for showing some photographs of you and your families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather, David Rosenson, and this picture was taken probably in the late '20s or very early '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my mother, Hena Rosenson Finkelstein. This photo was taken probably in the '30s in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my father, Schmuel Finkelstein. This photo was taken in Poland, probably 1930. Do you know how old he was [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- at that time he was probably 20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Regina Goldstein at the age of approximately two, taken in Poland in 1937. Should I say late 1920s or 1930?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's probably the early '30s.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll say 1932.

speaker: interviewee
text: Around.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, there we go. Here we go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This-- this is a picture of my-- I think this is a cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left in the front?

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left in the back. In the center is my mother, Hena Rosenson, her sister Franca Rosenson, Chaya Rosenson, who's my mother now and has been for many years. And this also is another cousin. And I believe the picture was taken in Poland around 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: The things we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Identify-- OK, here we go.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of my kindergarten class taken in Poland, probably 1938. And my sister Gloria and I at the bottom to the-- [? To the ?] right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the right, as far as we know, are the only survivors from this group.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Describe what your sister-- what you're wearing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm wearing a little dress. My braids are down the front with a couple of ribbons. And my sister Gloria is on my--

speaker: interviewee
text: Left.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on my right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So this is you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's Gloria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's right. That's left.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's you. That's Gloria.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Correct. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my uncle Chaim Rosenson. This picture was taken in the early-- in the 1930s, closely before World War II. He was in the Polish Army, and he died a hero's death as a partisan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's a good point though. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my uncle, Sana Rosenson. This picture was taken in Israel in 1990. He was very young and a partisan for three years. He fought with the Russian partisans all the way across Ukraine and into the Carpathian Mountains, and he was decorated by Khrushchev. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and father, Helen and Harry Kotlar in the company of Menachem Begin. And as I told you, our family are Zionists from way back when. And this was taken on one of Mr. Menachem Begin's visits to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do we-- do we know what year? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? In the '80s. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Early '80s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably in the early '80s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go. This is my wedding picture, Hal and Regina Goldstein on their wedding day, January 21, 1956. And then these guys.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Susan, my son, Jeffrey. This picture was taken in Hollywood in 1950-- 1966.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Hollywood--

speaker: interviewee
text: California.

speaker: interviewer
text: United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, United States. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Show me this guy and the other guy. [LAUGHTER] And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Hal, my daughter, Susan, my son, Jeff, and I at my parents' golden wedding anniversary. That was in 1984-- '86.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Beverly Hills, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Beverly Hills, California. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the five immigrants, my mother, Helen, my sister Betty, my father, Harry, my sister Gloria, and I, Regina. Hanukkah party at Betty's house, 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Encino, California. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an [INAUDIBLE] book, a memorial book to the people of the town where I was born, Kurow, Poland. It was published in 1955. And there are stories of the survivors of our town there and some of the families that were already in the United States or elsewhere. But it's all the people who come from my birth town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who left you this book?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lady by the name of [? Mindala-- ?] whose last name I can't remember, God forgive me-- left it to me in her will, because there were not enough to go around to the younger generation. This is a story that my uncle wrote, his story for this memorial book. And this is a picture of me taken around 19-- a citizenship paper, citizenship paper picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which uncle wrote this story?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle Sana Rosenson, who lives in Israel today, the partisan. I got to get that word in.
